metadata.title: Find All Numbers Disappeared in an Array

learning.whatWillYouLearn: Prerequisites: Arrays, Loops, Index Manipulation, Time Complexity, Space Complexity
learning.whyDoesThisExist: Imagine students are assigned roll numbers from 1 to n. During attendance, some students answer twice while others are absent. By marking the roll numbers that appear, the unmarked roll numbers represent the absent students.
learning.realWorldAnalogy: Imagine students are assigned roll numbers from 1 to n. During attendance, some students answer twice while others are absent. By marking the roll numbers that appear, the unmarked roll numbers represent the absent students.
learning.whenToUseThis: Use this Index Marking pattern whenever array values are guaranteed to lie in the range [1, n] and you need O(1) extra space.
learning.signals: Whenever array values are restricted to the range [1,n], consider using the array indices themselves as storage.
learning.expectedLearningOutcome: I will know when to use this pattern.

problem.problemStatement: Given an integer array nums of length n where every integer is in the range [1, n], some elements appear twice and others appear once. Return all the integers in the range [1, n] that do not appear in nums.

examples.explanation: Numbers 5 and 6 never appear in the array.

edgeCases: Missing numbers at the beginning or end

thinkingProcess.naiveIdea: For every number from 1 to n, search the entire array to determine whether it exists.
thinkingProcess.keyInsight: Since every value belongs to the range [1, n], every number has a corresponding index. Mark that index as visited. After processing the array, every index that remains unmarked corresponds to a missing number.
thinkingProcess.ahaMoment: Every value corresponds to exactly one index because values lie in the range [1,n].
thinkingProcess.patternRecognition: Whenever array values are restricted to the range [1,n], consider using the array indices themselves as storage.
thinkingProcess.whyItWorks: Each element is processed twice. The input array itself stores visitation information, eliminating the need for extra memory.
thinkingProcess.howToRecognizeSimilar: Whenever array values are restricted to the range [1,n], consider using the array indices themselves as storage.
commonWrongPaths: Forgetting to use abs() while indexing.
Using value instead of value-1 as the index.
Negating an already negative number without checking.

hints: Every value maps to an index.
Use the input array itself for marking.
Positive indices after traversal indicate missing numbers.
commonMisconceptions: Forgetting to use abs() while indexing.
Using value instead of value-1 as the index.
Negating an already negative number without checking.

pattern.name: Index Marking
pattern.recognitionSignals: Whenever array values are restricted to the range [1,n], consider using the array indices themselves as storage.

approaches.title: Linear Search for Every Number
approaches.idea: For every number from 1 to n, search the entire array to determine whether it exists.
algorithm: If the number is not found, add it to the answer.
approaches.dryRun:
nums=[4,3,2,7,8,2,3,1]
Search for 1,2,3,4,5...
5 not found
6 not found

code.java: Brute force omitted because every value requires a complete search.

approaches.title: HashSet
approaches.idea: Store every number inside a HashSet, then iterate from 1 to n and collect the numbers that are not present.
algorithm: Insert every number into a HashSet.
If a number is missing from the set, add it to the answer.

approaches.title: Index Marking (Negative Marking)
approaches.idea: Since values belong to the range [1,n], use each value as an index. Traverse the array and mark the corresponding position negative. Afterward, every positive index represents a missing number.
approaches.intuition: Each element is processed twice. The input array itself stores visitation information, eliminating the need for extra memory.
algorithm: Mark nums[index] as negative if it is positive.
Every positive position i means number i+1 is missing.
approaches.dryRun:
nums=[4,3,2,7,8,2,3,1]
Mark indices:
4→3
3→2
2→1
7→6
8→7
...
Remaining positive positions: 4,5
Answer=[5,6]

visualization.description: Each value marks its corresponding index as visited.
steps: Positive indices after traversal are missing numbers.

interviewFollowUps: Can this be solved without modifying the input array?
How does this differ from Find All Duplicates?
Can Cyclic Sort also solve this problem?
What if values are outside the range [1,n]?
How would you find both missing and duplicate numbers?
interviewTips: Explain why the value range enables index marking.
Remember to use abs() after marking values negative.
State O(n) time and O(1) extra space.
Mention Cyclic Sort as an alternative solution.

cheatSheet.keyObservation: Every value corresponds to exactly one index because values lie in the range [1,n].
cheatSheet.algorithm: Mark Index → Scan Positive Indices → Return Missing Numbers.

commonMistakes: Forgetting to use abs() while indexing.
Using value instead of value-1 as the index.
Negating an already negative number without checking.

relatedProblems: Find All Duplicates in an Array
